network.overall_strategy: The network follows the literature consensus that abiotic chemistry robustly generates racemic alanine through multiple convergent routes, while L-alanine is achieved only indirectly through a biochemical/prebiotic symmetry-breaking and amplification module rather than a single direct enantioselective synthesis step. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies formate, formaldehyde, pyruvate, and direct amino-acid products on Fe/S and Ni/Fe mineral systems; surface chemistry supplies acetaldehyde, aminonitriles, hydantoins, HCN oligomer products, and spark-generated precursors; biochemical settings then convert racemic alanine into an L-enriched pool through network-level selection consistent with CISS-informed and autocatalytic selection concepts discussed in the literature. Ten distinct pathways are encoded, converging mainly at HCN, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, alpha-aminopropionitrile, and DL-alanine.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S phases yields surface-bound formyl equivalents released as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe-S catalysis and vent-associated CO2 reduction to simple C1 organics are supported by iron-sulfur world and alkaline vent studies; formate is a plausible early product and network entry point for hydrothermal carbon fixation (Wachtershauser, 1988; Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Fe(Ni)S-mediated hydrothermal formation of formaldehyde from CO2 and H2

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent, pH 9-11, proton gradient across mineral barriers

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise reduction of CO2 on Fe(Ni)S-type mineral surfaces generates reduced C1 oxygenates including formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde production from H2 and CO2 under vent-like Fe(Ni)S catalysis is explicitly tied to alkaline hydrothermal vent chemistry and formaldehyde generation in vent reactors (Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Magnetite/Fe-Ni promoted hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from CO2

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal/subsurface setting; rTCA-like carbon fixation network

reactions.mechanism: Multistep mineral-promoted CO2 reduction and C-C bond formation in rTCA-like chemistry yields pyruvate as a central alpha-keto acid.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a key hydrothermal branch point supported by Fe/Ni nanoparticle CO2 reduction and iron-promoted reverse TCA chemistry; this node is retained as a network abstraction of those experimentally observed multistep systems rather than a single elementary step (Muchowska et al., 2019; Beyazay et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Spark discharge generation of HCN and acetaldehyde from reduced atmospheric gases

conditions.other: electric discharge in CH4/NH3/H2/H2O or volcanic CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S atmosphere; products dissolve into surface waters

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates reactive carbon-nitrogen species and aldehydes in the atmosphere; rainout and mineral concentration deliver HCN and acetaldehyde to aqueous pools.
reactions.reasoning: Miller-Urey chemistry produces HCN and acetaldehyde in situ, with alanine subsequently arising through Strecker chemistry; volcanic and H2S-containing variants increase amino-acid output substantially (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011). Mineral agents here function as required surface concentration/adsorption media after atmospheric synthesis.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox formation of acetaldehyde from HCN-derived carbon feedstocks

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox with mineral phosphate and sulfide context
conditions.other: UV irradiation, H2S present, near-neutral pH

reactions.mechanism: Reductive homologation in cyanosulfidic networks converts HCN-derived carbon intermediates into simple aldehydes including acetaldehyde under UV photoredox conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Sutherland-group cyanosulfidic chemistry generates aldehyde precursors required for amino-acid synthesis, and acetaldehyde is specifically identified as accessible by photoredox homologation feeding alanine chemistry (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker aminonitrile formation from acetaldehyde

conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions, acidic or basic pH, concentrated NH3 and HCN

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde forms an imine with ammonia, followed by cyanide addition to produce alpha-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the classical Strecker route to alanine precursors and the best-established directed route to alanine from acetaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN (Strecker, 1850; Miller, 1955; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker synthesis of alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: UV irradiation, H2S present, neutral pH, reported ~70% aminonitrile yield from acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Phosphoro-Strecker/cyanosulfidic photochemistry channels acetaldehyde, NH3, and HCN to alpha-aminopropionitrile under UV-driven redox cycling.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. reported alanine precursor formation at about 70% aminonitrile yield from acetaldehyde under cyanosulfidic, phosphate-containing photoredox conditions, making this a distinct and strongly supported branch to the same hub intermediate (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile to racemic alanine

conditions.other: sequential hydrolysis via alaninamide under mild aqueous conditions

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis proceeds through amide intermediates and terminates in racemic 2-aminopropanoic acid.
reactions.reasoning: The accepted Strecker pathway to alanine proceeds through alpha-aminopropionitrile and alaninamide before yielding DL-alanine; racemic outcome is expected (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Pyrite-coupled reductive amination of pyruvate to racemic alanine

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal setting; oxidative pyrite formation provides reducing equivalents

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes imine formation with ammonia followed by mineral-coupled reduction to alanine as pyrite forms from FeS and H2S.
reactions.reasoning: Hafenbradl et al. demonstrated prebiotic reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine coupled to oxidative pyrite formation, directly paralleling biosynthetic logic in iron-sulfur chemistry (Hafenbradl et al., 1995).

reactions.name: Mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.other: alkaline conditions, maximum yield near 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III)

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate is aminated and reduced on mixed-valence iron mineral surfaces under alkaline vent-compatible conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. showed pyruvate-to-alanine conversion under alkaline conditions with mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides, strongest at 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III), linking vent sediments to prebiotic amino-acid synthesis (Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Ni-catalyzed H2-driven reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.other: pH 7-8, 30 mM pyruvate, biomimetic H2 reduction

reactions.mechanism: Nickel-catalyzed hydrogenation of the pyruvate-derived imine/amino intermediate affords alanine under mild aqueous conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur, Rauscher, and Werner demonstrated Ni-catalyzed H2-driven reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine at 22C, pH 7-8, and 5 bar H2; the mineral agents listed reflect hydrothermal metal context while the catalytic condition follows the study (Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs cyclization from pyruvate to 5-methylhydantoin

conditions.catalyst: none beyond concentrated aqueous mineral setting
conditions.other: cyanide/ammonia chemistry with CO2 capture; warm aqueous conditions

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate reacts with cyanide and ammonia to form an aminonitrile-type intermediate, with decarboxylation-derived CO2 captured intramolecularly to furnish 5-methylhydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs-type route from pyruvate through 5-methylhydantoin is specifically documented as a prebiotic alanine pathway bridging amino-acid and pyrimidine chemistry (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine
reactions.mechanism: Hydantoin ring opening under warm aqueous conditions yields the corresponding N-carbamoyl amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. identified N-carbamoyl alanine as the downstream hydrolysis product of 5-methylhydantoin en route to alanine under 80C conditions (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to racemic alanine
reactions.mechanism: Carbamoyl cleavage releases free alanine from the N-carbamoyl intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The warm aqueous Bucherer-Bergs sequence proceeds from hydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine to alanine and is directly relevant to prebiotic alanine synthesis (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN in concentrated eutectic or evaporitic settings

conditions.catalyst: concentration by eutectic freezing or drying
conditions.other: HCN concentration >0.01 M; eutectic freezing can concentrate to ~25 M

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN undergoes oligomerization through dimeric and trimeric nitrile intermediates to the tetramer DAMN.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is the canonical HCN tetramer and a major branch point in concentrated HCN chemistry; such concentrations are plausible in eutectic ice or evaporitic concentration regimes (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer material to racemic alanine-containing amino acid mixture

conditions.catalyst: alkaline polymerization followed by hydrolytic workup
conditions.other: represents hydrolysis of DAMN-derived/polymeric HCN black material rather than single clean stoichiometric conversion

reactions.mechanism: DAMN participates in broader HCN polymer chemistry; subsequent hydrolysis of the polymeric material releases mixed amino acids including alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports alanine detection after hydrolysis of concentrated HCN oligomer/polymer material rather than a clean DAMN-only elementary step; this node is therefore an explicit network abstraction of the experimentally observed polymer-hydrolysis route (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal NiFeS amino-acid synthesis yielding alanine among products

conditions.other: 10 days; CO/KCN/CH3SH-type hydrothermal synthesis represented by CO and cyanide hub connectivity

reactions.mechanism: Ni,Fe sulfide-mediated hydrothermal carbonyl/cyanide chemistry generates a product suite including alpha-amino acids such as alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Huber and Wachtershauser reported amino-acid formation, including alanine, in hydrothermal Ni,Fe sulfide systems from CO/KCN/CH3SH under 80-120C conditions; this reaction is intentionally represented as a direct product-diverse hydrothermal source rather than over-detailed unsupported substeps (Huber and Wachtershauser, 2006).

reactions.name: UV ice-analog photochemistry yielding racemic alanine after warming/hydrolysis

conditions.temperature: 12K photolysis then warming to aqueous workup
conditions.catalyst: UV irradiation of H2O:CH3OH:NH3:HCN ice analogs
conditions.other: alanine detected at about 5% of glycine yield after hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Photolysis of mixed ice analogs generates complex organics that release amino acids, including alanine, upon warming and hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Alanine formation in UV-processed interstellar ice analogs is well documented, though at lower yield than glycine; this route broadens the network with extraterrestrially plausible but surface-accessible feedstock processing (Bernstein et al., 2002; Muñoz Caro et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic symmetry breaking and amplification from DL-alanine pool to L-alanine pool

conditions.catalyst: network-level selection at mineral interfaces and protocell-like compartments
conditions.other: indirect symmetry breaking and amplification; not a single established elementary reaction

reactions.mechanism: A small initial enantiomeric imbalance in broader prebiotic networks is amplified through coupled selection, crystallization/eutectic partitioning, peptide-ligation feedback, and possible RNA-mediated stereoselection to yield an L-enriched alanine pool.
reactions.reasoning: The literature does not support a single direct conversion of DL-alanine to pure L-alanine. Instead, it points to indirect systems-level routes involving CISS-related symmetry breaking, homochiral RNA selection, crystallization/eutectic amplification, and autocatalytic network effects. This node is therefore explicitly modeled as a biochemical selection module rather than a discrete enantioselective synthesis reaction (Ozturk and Sasselov, 2022; Ozturk et al., 2023; Ozturk et al., 2025; Blackmond et al., 2025; Breslow and Levine, 2006; Klussmann et al., 2006).

pathways.name: Classic spark-Strecker route
pathways.description: Atmospheric discharge supplies HCN and acetaldehyde, which undergo classical Strecker chemistry and hydrolysis to alanine, followed by indirect L-selection.

pathways.name: Volcanic/H2S-enhanced spark-Strecker route
pathways.description: Same core Miller chemistry as P1 but representing sulfur-rich volcanic discharge conditions with elevated alanine formation before biochemical L-selection.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde to spark-assisted Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal vents furnish formaldehyde, which feeds surface atmospheric photochemistry/discharge producing acetaldehyde and HCN, then Strecker chemistry to alanine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN-derived aldehyde chemistry under UV/H2S/phosphate conditions gives acetaldehyde, then alpha-aminopropionitrile at high yield, then alanine.

pathways.name: Pyrite-driven hydrothermal reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction furnishes pyruvate, which is converted to alanine by pyrite-coupled reductive amination, then enters L-selection.

pathways.name: Mixed-valence iron pyruvate amination route
pathways.description: Pyruvate formed in hydrothermal carbon-fixation chemistry is aminated on mixed Fe(II)/Fe(III) minerals in alkaline vent-compatible settings.

pathways.name: Ni/H2 biomimetic pyruvate amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is converted to alanine under mild Ni/H2 catalysis, matching the modern-biosynthetic analog route before L-selection.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route from pyruvate
pathways.description: Pyruvate, ammonia, cyanide, and CO2 yield 5-methylhydantoin, then N-carbamoyl alanine, then alanine, linking amino-acid and nucleobase precursor chemistry.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN/polymeric material that releases alanine upon hydrolysis, then feeds the L-selection module.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal NiFeS direct amino-acid synthesis route
pathways.description: CO/cyanide hydrothermal sulfide chemistry directly yields alanine among products; the racemic pool then enters biochemical L-selection.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong classic spark-Strecker logic and good literature alignment for racemic alanine production. The main weakness is that the pathway only robustly reaches DL-alanine; conversion to L-alanine relies on a speculative symmetry-breaking module not yet experimentally established for alanine itself.
suggestions: Reframe the endpoint as racemic alanine with optional L-enrichment rather than direct L-alanine synthesis
Specify a concrete amplification setting for rxn_019, e.g. magnetite/CISS-informed selection plus crystallization or peptide-coupled selection
Include explicit hydrolysis intermediates such as alaninamide if aiming for tighter Strecker realism

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The H2S-enhanced spark/cyanosulfidic-Strecker route is well supported for aminonitrile formation and is one of the strongest routes to DL-alanine. As with other pathways, the final chiral enrichment step is the dominant uncertainty.
suggestions: Add Cu photoredox and phosphate explicitly to better match Patel et al. conditions
Separate spark generation and cyanosulfidic conversion more clearly if these are meant to be distinct stages
Treat L-alanine as an enriched pool produced by network selection, not a discrete high-yield single-step product

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway nicely demonstrates hydrothermal-to-surface interconnection via formaldehyde feeding atmospheric/surface chemistry. However, direct hydrothermal formaldehyde formation from CO2 and H2 is plausible but less secure than the best-supported alanine-forming steps, and final L-selection remains speculative.
suggestions: Strengthen the formaldehyde step with explicit Fe(Ni)S vent conditions from Herschy et al.
Clarify whether formaldehyde is exported physically to the surface or reprocessed locally
If possible, add acetaldehyde generation through a literature-supported route more directly linked to formaldehyde photochemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The phosphoro-Strecker/cyanosulfidic step to alpha-aminopropionitrile is literature-aligned and strong. The weaker point is the route used to make acetaldehyde from HCN plus formate, which is underspecified relative to the literature that more often treats aldehyde generation separately.
suggestions: Replace or better specify rxn_005 using a more directly supported acetaldehyde-forming photoredox sequence
Explicitly include Cu(I)/Cu(II), H2S, UV, and phosphate agents for the cyanosulfidic module
Lower confidence claims for direct L-alanine formation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A strong hydrothermal route grounded in reductive amination literature. Pyruvate-to-alanine on pyrite-like Fe/S systems is one of the best-supported abiotic alanine syntheses. The main limitation is again that the pathway stops securely at racemic alanine.
suggestions: Specify pyrite-forming FeS/H2S coupling conditions more explicitly
Indicate realistic temperatures below major alanine decomposition thresholds
Present L-enrichment as downstream selection from a racemic pool

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the best pathways in the network. Mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide-driven pyruvate amination is directly literature-supported under alkaline vent-relevant conditions. As elsewhere, enantioenrichment to L-alanine is the unresolved step.
suggestions: Include the key 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III) condition from the literature
Clarify pH 9 vent sediment context
Consider ending the validated pathway at DL-alanine and appending a lower-confidence L-selection module

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent alignment for pyruvate reductive amination under Ni/H2 conditions, especially because the literature gives unusually concrete parameters. The only reason this falls short of approval is the unsupported jump from DL-alanine to L-alanine as a discrete biochemical node.
suggestions: State the literature-matched conditions explicitly: pH 7-8, 22 C, 5 bar H2
Justify transport from hydrothermal pyruvate formation to milder Ni-catalyzed settings if these are distinct locales
Decouple proven racemic synthesis from hypothetical symmetry breaking

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a very coherent literature-aligned Bucherer-Bergs route and one of the best-designed pathways in the network. The hydantoin and N-carbamoyl alanine intermediates are important strengths. The residual weakness is the speculative chiral amplification step.
suggestions: Add the 80 C hydrolysis condition explicitly for the hydantoin-to-alanine conversion
Mention the associated dihydroorotate linkage if emphasizing systems chemistry relevance
Keep the validated product as DL-alanine unless an explicit amplification mechanism is modeled

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN oligomerization to DAMN is well supported, but hydrolysis of oligomeric HCN material gives complex amino acid mixtures rather than a clean alanine-producing step. This pathway is plausible as a minor contributor but too nonspecific to support confident target-directed synthesis.
suggestions: Represent rxn_016 as producing an amino acid mixture rather than alanine alone
If retained, lower its weight in the network and mark it as low-selectivity
Provide concentration/eutectic freezing justification for HCN polymerization

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrothermal NiFeS amino acid synthesis is literature-supported as a product mixture, but modeling it as a direct, target-specific alanine reaction overstates selectivity. It is best treated as a background source of racemic alanine among multiple products, not a clean pathway to L-alanine.
suggestions: Model rxn_017 as yielding an amino acid mixture including alanine
Specify the actual feedstocks more faithfully if using Huber and Wachtershauser-style chemistry
Avoid presenting this route as a direct selective source of L-alanine

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a strong, well-connected synthesis network for prebiotic alanine chemistry and it aligns well with the literature on racemic alanine formation. Topologically, it is dense and connected, with credible hubs at HCN, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, aminonitrile, and DL-alanine, and multiple convergent routes into pyruvate-derived and Strecker-derived alanine. Environmental interconnection is also good: hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon intermediates and pyruvate, surface chemistry generates HCN/acetaldehyde/aminonitriles/hydantoins, and a final biochemical module attempts chirality selection. The major weakness is target definition: the literature strongly supports robust synthesis of DL-alanine, not direct L-alanine. Thus the network convincingly achieves racemic alanine but only hypothesizes L-enrichment through rxn_019. That final step is conceptually consistent with the literature's conclusion that alanine homochirality must emerge at the network level rather than by direct asymmetric synthesis, but the specific implementation is underdetermined and not experimentally established for alanine. Additional concerns are that some reactions are overly compressed or too selective relative to the underlying studies: rxn_005 is underspecified, rxn_016 should yield a mixture rather than alanine specifically, and rxn_017 overstates direct alanine selectivity from hydrothermal NiFeS chemistry. Also, because the supplied network summary omits detailed reaction metadata, it is impossible to verify that every hydrothermal and surface reaction actually names required mineral agents in the formal schema. Overall, this is a good network for 'prebiotic alanine synthesis with possible later L-enrichment', but not yet an approved network for direct synthesis of L-alanine.
